lesson_title: Introduction to programs and programming languages

cards.back: A sequence of instructions that tells the computer what to do. Programs are written by programmers using programming languages.
cards.source: 0.2 - Introduction to programs and programming languages

cards.text: The physical components of a computer (keyboard, monitor, CPU) are called {{c1::hardware}}, while the programs and data are called {{c2::software}}.
cards.source: 0.2 - Introduction to programs and programming languages

cards.front: What is the primary function of the CPU?
cards.back: The CPU (Central Processing Unit) executes program instructions. It performs calculations, makes decisions, and controls the flow of the program.
cards.source: 0.2 - Introduction to programs and programming languages

cards.text: CPUs can only understand {{c1::machine language}} (also called {{c2::machine code}}), which consists of {{c3::binary}} instructions (1s and 0s).
cards.source: 0.2 - Introduction to programs and programming languages

cards.concept: Why programmers don't write in machine language
cards.explanation: Machine language is extremely difficult for humans to read and write. It consists of binary numbers that are hard to understand, debug, and maintain. Each CPU architecture has different machine language instructions.
cards.source: 0.2 - Introduction to programs and programming languages

cards.front: What is the difference between low-level and high-level programming languages?
cards.back: Low-level languages (assembly) are close to machine code and CPU-specific. High-level languages (C++, Python) are more human-readable, portable, and abstract away hardware details.
cards.source: 0.2 - Introduction to programs and programming languages

cards.text: {{c1::Assembly language}} uses short mnemonic names for instructions (like {{c2::mov}}, {{c3::add}}, {{c4::sub}}) instead of binary numbers.
cards.source: 0.2 - Introduction to programs and programming languages

cards.front: Why can't you run x86 assembly code directly on an ARM processor?
cards.back: Each CPU architecture has its own machine language and assembly language. Code written for one architecture won't work on another without rewriting or translation.
cards.source: 0.2 - Introduction to programs and programming languages

cards.text: C++ is a {{c1::high-level}} programming language that is {{c2::portable}} across different CPU architectures when recompiled.
cards.source: 0.2 - Introduction to programs and programming languages

cards.front: What are three main advantages of high-level languages over assembly?
cards.back:
• Easier to read and write (more like natural language)
  • Portable across different CPU architectures
  • Faster to develop and maintain programs
cards.source: 0.2 - Introduction to programs and programming languages

cards.back: The programs and data that run on computer hardware
cards.source: 0.2 - Introduction to programs and programming languages

cards.front: What is a statement in a programming language?
cards.back: A statement is a single instruction in a program that tells the computer to perform a specific action. Programs consist of sequences of statements.
cards.source: 0.2 - Introduction to programs and programming languages
